So this is a forty-nine-year-old man who presented with a recurrent ventral incisional hernia, surgical history notable for an exploratory laparotomy with small bowel resection, and then a robotic eye palm plus.
Cross-sectional imaging demonstrated a five by eleven centimeter M two M three hernia.
The surgeon observes adhesions of the bowel to the previously placed polypropylene mesh, although the mesh itself is not visibly distinct.
We planned for an initial left lateral doc with a possible mesh explant.
Upon entry, dense adhesions of the bowel to the mesh are visible, with the mesh appearing well incorporated into the anterior abdominal wall.
The surgeon performs lysis of adhesions, carefully separating the bowel from the mesh and anterior abdominal wall, which is a critical step in the operation.

The surgeon uses a force bipolar instrument to carefully manipulate tissue around the bowel, ensuring not to grasp the bowel directly, while applying tension and counter-tension to aid in the dissection process.
So this is a process that can take some time and in this case took about maybe fifteen minutes of surgical time.
The surgeon performs lysis of adhesions, carefully separating dense adhesions between the bowel and the mesh incorporated with the anterior abdominal wall.
The surgeon focuses on releasing the bowel from the anterior abdominal wall, carefully separating it from the dense adhesions observed upon entry.
The surgeon visually assesses the mesh, noting its well-incorporated appearance, and determines that mesh explantation is not necessary.
So the mesh was not explanted in anticipation that it would be quite destructive to the abdominal wall and may result in a higher burden for more complex reconstruction.
The surgeon leaves the mesh in situ and proceeds with retromuscular dissection, carefully separating the muscle layers.
The surgeon initiates dissection by mobilizing the falciform ligament, preparing it for use in the eventual closure of the posterior layer.
The surgeon identifies the linea alba and makes an incision just beyond it to access the contralateral retro-rectus space.
The surgeon proceeds with a standard retro-rectus dissection, continuing the surgical approach within the retro-rectus space.
The surgeon develops the initial entry into the retroactive space, grasping the posterior sheath and continuing the retromuscular dissection towards the patient's semilunar line.
The surgeon observes the limited retroactive space on the patient's right side, noting the constraints in the area during the dissection.
And I was a little worried that given the size of the hernia and the recurrent nature of the hernia, that the mesh coverage would be inadequate if exclusively using a retrorectus dissection.
And so a question that often comes up is what do we do when we anticipate posterior tension and or inadequate mesh coverage? many surgeons will resort to a TAR.
And though I think it's great for anterior tension, I think that it may be overutilized in the MIS setting.
When thinking about the technical elements of performing TAR, we oftentimes think about a top-down approach where we enter and intersect the TA and extend downwards.

The Madrid modification of the transversus abdominis release is a muscle sparing approach that I personally like to use whenever I want extra coverage without necessarily needing more anterior fascial medialization.
I think it's great with less neurovascular injury and less potential for semi-literal line disruption.

The surgeon begins the dissection medially within the preperitoneal fat pad, avoiding the identification of the posterior lamella over the transversus abdominis.

The surgeon reflects the peritoneum and the peritoneal fat pad downward while simultaneously reflecting the posterior sheath of the rectus muscle upward, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon identifies fibers of the transversus abdominis muscle, visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon performs a transection of approximately one to two centimeters of the transversus abdominis muscle, visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon continues a muscle-sparing dissection, avoiding transection of the transversus abdominis (TA), while working posterior to the muscle.
The surgeon performs the Madrid modification of the transversus abdominis release, focusing on the preperitoneal fat pad and reflecting the peritoneum and posterior sheath of the rectus.

Many surgeons, when they observe this case, will wonder whether or not it's just an extended preperitoneal dissection, much like Dr. Todd Hannaford out of the Carolinas Medical Center has promoted and popularized.
And I think that that's a fair statement and a very similar dissection strategy here.
The surgeon dissects towards the semilunar line, incises the posterior sheath, and enters the preperitoneal space, performing a preperitoneal dissection to expand the surgical area.
our posterior closure, but also provide a larger space for mesh coverage.
So many benefits gained here without transecting the transversus abdominis and reducing the neurovascular injury associated with a transversus abdominis release.
The surgeon extends the preperitoneal dissection towards the retroperitoneum, revealing the anatomical layers and structures encountered during this process.
The retroinguinal space is visible on the left side, with trochars placed for double docking to facilitate contralateral dissection.
The surgeon observes the contralateral retrorectus space, noting the presence of more sheath, which negates the need for an extended dissection on this side.
The surgeon begins placating the patient's abdominal wall well cephalad to the hernia defect, aiming to bring the posterior sheath together after aligning the anterior sheath.

The surgeon closes the ventral hernia defect using a number one permanent V-lock suture, sequentially tightening the abdominal wall and placing it on traction. Multiple suture lines are applied with continuous tension to achieve closure without excessive force.
The surgeon re-approximates the rectus muscle caudal to the hernia defect, followed by closing the cephalic component, completing the reconstruction of the patient's linea alba.
The surgeon places a mesh into the surgical field, utilizing contralateral ports, before closing the posterior defect.

The surgeon closes the posterior sheath and visceral sac, ensuring the structures re-approximate without undue tension. The release and dissection allow for a secure closure, minimizing the risk of posterior fascial dehiscence.
The surgeon places a 26 by 28 cm mesh over the defect, ensuring broad coverage within the retromuscular space.

Patient did great with the same-day discharge. I feel that the Madrid modification allows for tension-free visceral sac closure without requiring a transversus abdominis release. Thank you very much, and I hope that was educational.
